author: David Benjamin
message_subject: Add a constant-time binary extended GCD algorithm.
message_body:
Thankfully, most modular inverses involve a prime or at least odd
modulus, but RSA key generation requires inverting with an even modulus
(the totient). Implementing that in constant-time is tricky.

This file adapts the binary extended GCD algorithm in the Handbook of
Applied Cryptography. This is to verify the C implementation in
https://boringssl-review.googlesource.com/c/boringssl/+/26370.

author: Samuel Tian
message_subject: compile reduce_full' with Z*Z instead of zrange

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add more operations to parsing in the equivalence checker (#1197)
message_body:
We now support parsing `call`, `cmovb`, `cmp`, `db`, `dd`, `dq`, `dw`,
`je`, `jmp`, `mul`, `pop`, `push`, `rcr`, and `shrx`, and prefixes like
`rep ret`, `repz ret`, `repnz ret`.  We also support labels, `align`,
and `default rel`.  We should probably try to properly support a larger
fraction of https://www.nasm.us/xdoc/2.15.05/html/nasmdoc3.html at some
point.

We now parse instructions (opcodes) in a case-insensitive manner, though
many other places are still case-sensitive.

The stack is now computed based on push and pop instructions as well as
the adjustments to rsp, and supports aggregating multiple constant-sized
adjustments to rsp.

We don't yet support the `0F3h` syntax alternative for the hex `0xF3`,
mostly because I couldn't be bothered to write a parser for it right
now.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Split off the non-bedrock-dependent part of equiv proofs
message_body:
This will hopefully make development a bit faster; these files are so
long.

message_body: Co-authored-by: Andres Erbsen <andreser@mit.edu>

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
Now `show` and `show_lines` no longer take a `with_parens` argument, and
instead that functionality is generalized into `ShowLevel` / `show_lvl`.
This is the first step towards having more flexibility in parenthesizing
printed expressions, in a more principled way.  It's sort-of a
test-drive of a more uniform way of level-based printing.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Pipe through an always-false equivalence checker
message_body:
This should be enough rope to check equivalence of PHOASTs with assembly
expressions.  It's plausible we want more control over the calling
conventions from the command line, for example, specifying:
- what to do when we run out of registers / how arguments are stored on
  the stack
- support for a number of common calling conventions

We may also want to emit better headers for the assembly files, rather
than replicating the header of the input file.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Copy proof from Coq 8.12 std lib for older versions of Coq

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
Note that we need to keep the individual lines of each function separate
so as to not stack-overflow when joining strings before printing,
because JSON has many, many more lines than most of the other languages.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Generate value_barrier wrappers for cmovznz code
message_body:
This is significant progress towards #748

Note that rather than checking for whether or not `OPENSSL_NO_ASM` is
defined, we instead check for the definition of a curve-specific
`*_NO_ASM` variable, where the prefix is the uppercased prefix of the
curve description.  This lets us not be tied to OpenSSL.  (I presume
that BoringSSL can do something like
```c
#ifdef OPENSSL_NO_ASM
#define FIAT_25519_NO_ASM
#define FIAT_P256_NO_ASM
[...]
#endif
```
)

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
We now compute the loose bounds by recording the number of additions
and/or balanced subtractions we want to be able to do in a row before we
need to multiply and carry.  Note that balanced subtraction always takes
a bit more overhead than addition.

This is a more conservative variant of #799 that doesn't actually change
the bounds, and makes the genuine change of #799 easier to see, namely,
reducing `headspace_add_count` from `2` to `1`.

message_body:
For
https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/issues/776#issuecomment-622970195

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
We can generate Java code for all of the 32-bit files.  We cannot
generate Java code for the 64-bit files because Java does not seem to
support uint128 nor 64x64 -> 64x64 multiplication.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Refactor the stringification and IR code a bit
message_body:
We now delegate the descriptions of when and where to cast to the
different languages, and let them have custom logic based on the
identifier.

This hopefully makes things a bit cleaner and more obvious, while also
making things more flexible.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add more heuristic definitions for unsat solinas
message_body:
We don't make use of any new ones yet, though.

Inspired by
https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/c60b1d2556a72c37f4bc7444204e9ddc0791ce4f/generate_parameters.py

I have copied the `< 2 * bitwidth` from
https://github.com/mit-plv/fiat-crypto/blob/c60b1d2556a72c37f4bc7444204e9ddc0791ce4f/generate_parameters.py#L233,
but I have no idea why we check against `2 * bitwidth` rather than
against `bitwidth`.

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
Currently we don't handle rules that require "concrete list of cons
cells" nor rules that require "concrete nat literal to do recursion on";
both of these require special treatment, to be implemented in an
upcoming commit.

The reifier is kind-of slow, alas.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Pass around lists of strings for error messages
message_body:
This allows us to get back an error message in Saturated Solinas in
OCaml on P256 x32 in reasonable time without blowing the stack.

There's the slight oddity that the list of string in the success case is
joined by the empty string, but the list of string in the error case is
joined by newline.  Probably meanas that I chose the wrong abstraction
barrier somewhere.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Preliminary support: conditional sub as primitive
message_body:
Things to be done:
- Fill in Axiom conditional_subtract_modulus in
  src/ModularArithmetic/ModularBaseSystemListZOperations.v (jadep)
- Refactor code to make GF25519.freeze use
  ModularBaseSystemListZOperations.conditional_subtract_modulus in a
  non-unfolded form (jadep)
- Check that the bounds I defined in conditional_subtract' in
  src/Reflection/Z/Interpretations.v are correct (jadep)
- Fill in bounds checking in conditional_subtract_o in
  src/Reflection/Z/Interpretations.v (jgross or jadep)
- Integrate boundedness lemma about conditional_subtract_modulus into
  BoundedWord64.conditional_subtract in src/Reflection/Z/Interpretations.v
  (jadep and jgross?)
- Prove BoundedWord64.invert_conditional_subtract (depends on some bits
  of the above bullet point, but could be done by jgross)
- Fill in the three admits in
  src/Reflection/Z/Interpretations/Relations.v (jadep or jgross?)
- Verify that everything works

(cc @jadephilipoom @andres-erbsen)

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Factor Reify_rhs so that it's more reusable

author: Jason Gross
message_body: The proof of wf-preservation for inline_const isn't finished yet, though...

author: Jason Gross
message_body:
We also have linearization of function application / lets,
constant-inlining, and reification.

This closes #58.

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add a generalized version of Barrett Reduction (#44)
message_body:
In this version, we split up the integer division so that we are less
likely to overflow in intermediate computations.

This is still not the version in HAC 14.42; that version also does early
reduction modulo b^(k+1).

This is work towards #43

author: Jason Gross
message_subject: Add documentation: Equality, HProp, Isomorphism, Sigma (#41)
message_body:
* Add doc: Equality, HProp, Isomorphism, Sigma

* Update documentation with suggestions from Andres

author: Jade Philipoom
message_subject: refactor of Basesystem and ModularBaseSystem; includes general code organization and changes to pseudomersenne base parameters that require bases to be expressed as powers of 2, which reduces the burden of proof on the caller and allows carry functions to use bitwise operations rather than mod and division
